message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  This study analyzes
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  =16384 COVID-19-related literature published between December 2019 to June 2020. The data were extracted from the Web of Science database using four keywords: “COVID-19”, “Coronavirus”, “2019-nCoV”, and “SARS-CoV-2”. The analysis found that almost all but a tiny number of the papers are published in 2020 (95.16%). Of the 15 types of publications,
                  <jats:italic>article</jats:italic>
                  (40.015%) is on the top of the list. All publications are in 19 different languages where English (95.313%) is the dominant one. A total of 159 countries produce COVID-19-related researches, and the USA (25.433%) is in the leading position. According to the findings, Wang Y (
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  =94) is the top author, and the British Medical Journal (BMJ) (
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  =488) is the top source. Also, the University of London (
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  =488) is the leading organization with the highest number of papers, and medicine-related papers (
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  =2259) are the highest in numbers. Apart from these novel findings, this study is perhaps the largest COVID-19-related bibliometric analysis to date.
                </jats:p>
title: Bibliometric analysis of COVID-19 literature

reference.unstructured: Chahrour, M. , Assi, S. , Bejjani, M. , Nasrallah, A. A. , Salhab, H. , Fares, M. Y. , & Khachfe, H. H. (2020). A Bibliometric Analysis of COVID-19 Research Activity: A Call for Increased Output. Cureus. https://doi.org/10.7759/cureus.7357

reference.article-title: Visual analysis of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) studies based on bibliometrics]
